medications: Hydroxyzine 25 mg as needed for acute anxiety (mostly evenings)
Melatonin 3 mg as needed for sleep
health.mental_health: Work-related anxiety has spiked ahead of a performance review; she’s been having intrusive worries about being fired and occasionally notices old eating-disorder urges returning under stress. She’s using a relapse-prevention plan with her therapist and dietitian but sleep has been inconsistent.
recent_visits: Telehealth therapy session (weekly) focused on anxiety and work stress
Outpatient dietitian follow-up for eating-disorder recovery (monthly)
Primary care visit for insomnia and reflux symptoms (late last month)

finance.debt: About $38k in federal student loans; carrying ~ $3.5k on a credit card after an emergency car repair and a few months of higher-than-usual spending during burnout. She’s worried about making rent if she’s terminated and has started looking into unemployment eligibility and short-term hardship options.
investments: Thrift Savings Plan (TSP) - target date fund
recent_transactions: Auto repair (brakes and tires) at a shop in Northeast D.C.
Rent payment for a one-bedroom apartment in Petworth
Metro SmartTrip reloads and occasional rideshare to late removals
Purchase of a used laptop to update case notes at home

relationships: Partner: Maya Thompson (dating ~2 years; supportive but strained by Lauren’s unpredictable hours and stress)
Best friend: Priya Shah (college friend in Arlington; main confidante about the performance review)
Older brother: Rohan Desai (lives in Baltimore; concerned about Lauren’s health and job stability)
Sponsor/peer mentor from an eating-disorder recovery support group: 'K.' (kept semi-anonymous for privacy)
Former field training officer: Denise Carter (mentor at work; relationship complicated by the review)
social.social_media_activity: Low-profile; mostly lurks on Instagram and local D.C. groups. She occasionally posts photos of museum visits or runs on the Mall, but has recently reduced posting to avoid coworkers speculating about her job situation.
private_conversations: Discussing the possibility of termination and whether to contact a union rep or attorney
Confiding in Priya about a panic episode before court and fear of relapse behaviors
Arguments with Maya about Lauren bringing work home and being emotionally shut down
Texting her brother about borrowing money if she loses her job (she feels ashamed)
Talking in recovery group about triggers related to control, food, and workplace scrutiny

schedule.work_hours: Typically Mon–Fri 8:30am–5:00pm, but frequently extends to evenings for emergency removals, hospital holds, and documentation; periodic on-call rotation (one week every 6–8 weeks).
regular_appointments: Weekly telehealth therapy on Wednesdays at 7:00pm
Monthly dietitian appointment (first Monday of the month during lunch)
Recovery support group meeting Sunday evenings
upcoming_events: Performance review meeting with supervisor and HR (scheduled for next Tuesday at 10:00am)
Court hearing for a neglect case (Friday morning)
Follow-up meeting with her mentor to review documentation standards (Thursday at 4:30pm)
Dinner with Maya’s friends (tentative; she may cancel due to anxiety)
schedule.daily_routine: Early Metro commute, prioritizes field visits and school/home checks during the day, then tries to complete documentation after hours. She sets alarms for meals and breaks as part of recovery, but stress often disrupts the routine.

professional.company: District of Columbia government agency (Child and Family Services / CPS unit)
projects: Managing an active caseload of ~14 families (safety plans, service referrals, and follow-up visits)
Coordinating with MPD, school counselors, and pediatric clinics for mandated reporting follow-ups
Preparing court reports and affidavits for two ongoing neglect cases
Implementing a documentation improvement plan after a recent internal audit
professional.performance_issues: She is under review for late case notes, missed internal documentation deadlines, and one instance of incomplete contact logging after a long overnight removal. She worries the review may lead to a Performance Improvement Plan or termination; the stress has impacted concentration and timeliness, though she has not had any substantiated misconduct.
workplace_relationships: Supervisor: Marisol Vega (supportive but firm; pushing compliance with documentation timelines)
Coworker: James O’Neal (often covers her field visits; resentment brewing about workload)
HR contact: Alina Brooks (scheduled for the review; makes Lauren feel scrutinized)
Court liaison: Thomas Nguyen (helpful but frustrated when reports arrive late)

hobbies: Long walks and occasional runs on the National Mall
Journaling and adult coloring to manage anxiety
Cooking simple meals and trying new vegetarian recipes
habits: Uses a meal-reminder app and keeps structured snacks to support eating-disorder recovery
Drinks 2–3 coffees on heavy field days (sometimes worsens anxiety)
Late-night case note catch-up on her personal laptop (blurs work-life boundaries)
Occasional vaping nicotine when highly stressed (she hides it from most people)
Avoids weighing herself and keeps bathroom scale out of the apartment as a recovery safeguard
lifestyle.living_situation: Rents a one-bedroom apartment in Petworth and lives alone; her partner stays over a few nights a week. She’s quietly considering taking on a roommate or moving if she loses her job.
lifestyle.dietary_preferences: Mostly vegetarian with a structured meal plan from her dietitian; avoids calorie counting and certain trigger foods, and tries to keep regular meals even when anxious or working late.
